replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes‑Benz S320 CDI / S350 CDI / BlueTEC Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect current S320 CDI / S350 CDI replacement costs. The OM642 V6 variant typically sits at the higher end of the range. See the Era Map above for generation‑specific pricing.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an S320 CDI / S350 CDI engine typically takes 12–16 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The OM642 oil cooler seal replacement is particularly labour‑intensive as many ancillary parts must be removed to access the cooler in the V of the engine.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: S‑Class S320 CDI / S350 CDI / BlueTEC — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Essential maintenance — catch before catastrophic failure
Piston‑valve contact means little is left to repair
Catch it early — prevents crankshaft damage
Replace or delete — prevents engine damage

buyingChecks.title: Buying This S320 CDI / S350 CDI / BlueTEC? Check These First
buyIf: * W220 with the OM648 straight‑six (considered one of Mercedes' most robust diesel engines)
* OM642 (W221/W222) with oil cooler seals replaced with updated seals
* OM642 (W221/W222) with documented timing chain inspection or replacement
* Full service history with regular oil changes — both engines are sensitive to oil quality
* No oil leaks around the V of the engine (oil cooler seal health — OM642)
* No excessive NVH or booming noises (harmonic balancer health — OM648)
* Evidence of regular longer runs (DPF health — BlueTEC models)
avoidIf: * OM642 with cold‑start rattle and no chain history — assume it's overdue
* OM642 with oil leaks in the V of the engine — oil cooler seals may be failing
* OM648 with excessive booming or droning noises — harmonic balancer may be failing
* No service history — both engines are unforgiving of neglect
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis — may indicate timing, injector or sensor problems
* BlueTEC models with AdBlue system faults — can be expensive to repair

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The S320 CDI and S350 CDI / BlueTEC represent Mercedes‑Benz's flagship diesel S‑Class offerings across two generations. The W220 S320 CDI (1999–2005) uses the OM648.960 — a 3.2‑litre straight‑six with a cast‑iron block, widely considered one of Mercedes' most robust diesel engines. The OM648 is generally a very reliable engine — with proper maintenance, examples have reached 150,000+ miles with no injector issues, negligible oil consumption and excellent fuel economy. Common issues include harmonic balancer wear (causing NVH) and occasional injector/glow plug problems. The W221/W222 S350 CDI / BlueTEC (2006–2017) uses the OM642 — a 3.0‑litre V6 with an aluminium block, producing up to 258 hp and 620 Nm torque. The OM642 is a powerful and refined engine but has well‑documented vulnerabilities: oil cooler seal leaks, timing chain stretch, swirl flap failures, and SBC pump failures on early cars. The SBC (Sensotronic Brake Control) pump on early W221 models is a known weak point that can be expensive to replace. With typical S‑Class values ranging from £5,000 (W220) to £25,000+ (W222), an engine replacement is a major financial commitment — a well‑maintained W220 OM648 or a W222 OM642 with documented oil cooler seal and timing chain history is the safest choice.

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes‑Benz S‑Class S320 CDI / S350 CDI / BlueTEC (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling or clattering (timing chain warning — both engines) — see the map above
* Oil Leaks — Check around the V of the engine for oil cooler seal leaks (OM642)
* Excessive NVH — Listen for booming, growling or droning noises (harmonic balancer wear — OM648)
* Service History — Including which engine era (OM648 or OM642) and oil‑change discipline
* Timing Chain — Check for any chain work or cold‑start rattle
* Injector Health — Check for smooth idle; any rough running may indicate injector issues
* Glow Plugs — Check for hard starting; seized glow plugs are common on OM642
* SBC Pump — For W221: check SBC pump health — can be an expensive repair
* AdBlue System — For BlueTEC models: check for warning lights or AdBlue faults
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes read; unresolved issues may indicate timing, injector or sensor problems
* MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures

items.question: How much does a Mercedes‑Benz S320 CDI / S350 CDI engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A reconditioned S320 CDI / S350 CDI engine typically costs £3,500–£6,000 supply only, with the OM642 V6 variant at the higher end. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,500 depending on the specialist. A full engine replacement on a Mercedes can range from £9,000 to £13,000. Reconditioned engines are available from specialist suppliers with 12‑24 month warranties. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your S‑Class.

items.question: Is the Mercedes‑Benz S320 CDI / S350 CDI engine reliable?
items.answer: It depends on which engine your S‑Class has. The W220 S320 CDI (1999–2005) uses the OM648.960 — a 3.2‑litre straight‑six with a cast‑iron block. It is widely considered one of Mercedes' most robust diesel engines — with proper maintenance, examples have reached 150,000+ miles with no injector issues. The OM642 (W221/W222 S350 CDI, 2006–2017) is a 3.0‑litre V6 with an aluminium block — a powerful and refined engine but with well‑documented vulnerabilities: oil cooler seal leaks, timing chain stretch, and swirl flap failures. With proper maintenance and proactive attention to known issues, both can be reliable.

items.question: What are the common problems with the Mercedes‑Benz S320 CDI / S350 CDI?
items.answer: S320 CDI (OM648): Harmonic balancer/crankshaft pulley wear (excessive NVH — booming, droning noises), injector and glow plug issues, and occasional timing chain wear. The OM648 is generally a very reliable engine. S350 CDI / BlueTEC (OM642): Oil cooler seal leaks (oil loss in the V of the engine — labour‑intensive repair), timing chain stretch (rattling on cold start — can destroy the engine if ignored), swirl flap failures, turbo actuator issues, EGR/DPF clogging, and SBC pump failure on early W221 models.

items.question: Is it worth replacing an S320 CDI / S350 CDI engine, or should I scrap the car?
items.answer: Depends on the vehicle value and engine era. Against a well‑kept W222 S350 CDI worth £15,000–£25,000+, a £5,000–£6,500 recon is 20–40% of the car's value — often worth it. Against a tired W220 with significant body issues, the same recon may exceed our 70% Mercedes‑Benz threshold — get a scrap valuation first. The OM648 is highly regarded for its robustness and is often preferred by enthusiasts.

items.question: How do I find my Mercedes‑Benz S320 CDI / S350 CDI engine code?
items.answer: W220 S320 CDI (1999–2005): OM648.960. W221/W222 S350 CDI / BlueTEC (2006–2017): OM642.930 or OM642.862. Check the VIN plate or vehicle documentation. The engine code is also stamped on the engine block. The S320 CDI produces 204 hp; the S350 CDI produces 235–258 hp depending on variant.

items.question: Can I repair the OM642 oil cooler seals, or do they need replacement?
items.answer: The oil cooler seals must be replaced — they are a known weakness on the OM642. Mercedes used seals of an inferior material that degrade over time. A replacement costs £1,000–£2,500. The repair is labour‑intensive as many ancillary parts must be removed to access the cooler in the V of the engine. Ignoring oil loss can lead to serious engine damage. Later models received improved seals, but the issue was not fully eliminated.
